candidates.description: Temperature characterizing the thermal energy of the core electron population
candidates.reason: subject=electron, base=temperature; radial sampling is not part of the name
candidates.docs_description: Mean kinetic energy per electron degree of freedom for the electron population, expressed as a thermodynamic temperature.
candidates.documentation:
Electron temperature $T_e$ represents the random kinetic energy of electrons after subtracting any mean flow, usually interpreted as the temperature of the local Maxwellian or near-Maxwellian part of the electron distribution. It controls collisional rates, resistivity, radiation balance, and the electron contribution to [electron pressure](name:electron_pressure), while comparison with [ion temperature](name:ion_temperature) indicates electron-ion thermal equilibration.

A kinetic definition is

$$
\frac{3}{2} e T_e = \left\langle \frac{1}{2} m_e |\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{u}_e|^2 \right\rangle
$$

where $e$ is the elementary charge, $m_e$ is electron mass, $\mathbf{v}$ is particle velocity, $\mathbf{u}_e$ is the mean electron flow velocity, and the brackets denote an average over the electron velocity distribution. For a Maxwellian plasma, the pressure relation is $p_e = n_e e T_e$, linking this quantity to [electron density](name:electron_density) and [electron pressure](name:electron_pressure).

Electron temperature is measured by Thomson scattering, inferred from electron cyclotron emission where the emission is optically thick, and estimated in edge plasmas with Langmuir probes or spectroscopic line-ratio methods. Representative values range from 0.5-20 keV in tokamak cores, can exceed 20 keV in high-performance scenarios, and fall to about 10-200 eV in the edge and scrape-off layer.

candidates.description: Temperature of the ion species, averaged over charge states when applicable
candidates.docs_description: Kinetic energy per degree of freedom of the ion population, expressed as an effective thermodynamic temperature.
candidates.documentation:
Ion temperature describes the random kinetic energy of ions after subtracting any mean flow velocity. It is a core transport quantity because ion heat conduction, ion pressure, collisional energy exchange, and fusion reactivity depend strongly on it; together with [ion_density](name:ion_density) it determines [ion_pressure](name:ion_pressure). For plasmas with several ion species, this name denotes the effective ion population temperature unless a species-resolved standard name is used.

For an isotropic ion velocity distribution, the temperature $T_i$ is the second central moment of the distribution:

$$
T_i = \frac{m_i}{3 e}\left\langle |\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{u}_i|^2 \right\rangle_i
$$

where $m_i$ is the ion mass, $e$ is the elementary charge, $\mathbf{v}$ is particle velocity, $\mathbf{u}_i$ is the mean ion flow velocity, and $\langle\cdot\rangle_i$ denotes an average over the ion distribution. The related [electron_temperature](name:electron_temperature) sets electron thermal pressure and collisional energy transfer, while differences between electron and ion temperatures indicate incomplete equilibration or strong auxiliary heating.

Ion temperature is measured with charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy, neutron spectroscopy in fusion plasmas, Doppler broadening of impurity lines, and neutral-particle analysis; it is also inferred from transport modeling constrained by power balance. Representative core values are 1-20 keV in present tokamak H-mode plasmas, rising above 20 keV in reactor-grade scenarios, while edge and scrape-off-layer values are often 10-500 eV.

candidates.description: Upper D-shape triangularity of a flux surface relative to the magnetic axis
candidates.reason: qualifier=upper, base=triangularity, intrinsic shape of flux_surface
candidates.docs_description: Dimensionless measure of upper-half D shaping of a magnetic flux surface, set by the inward radial shift of its highest point relative to the surface midplane center.
candidates.documentation:
Upper triangularity describes the D-shaped distortion of the upper half of a closed magnetic flux surface in the right-handed cylindrical $(R, \phi, Z)$ system, where $R$ is major radius and $Z$ is vertical height. It complements [lower triangularity](name:lower_triangularity_of_flux_surface) and is used with [elongation](name:elongation_of_flux_surface) to characterize equilibrium shaping.

A common equilibrium definition is

$$
\delta_u = \frac{R_c - R_{Z\max}}{a}
$$

where $\delta_u$ is the upper triangularity, $R_c=(R_{\rm out}+R_{\rm in})/2$ is the midplane center of the same flux surface, $a=(R_{\rm out}-R_{\rm in})/2$ is its [minor radius](name:minor_radius_of_flux_surface), and $R_{Z\max}$ is the major-radius coordinate of the uppermost point on the contour.

The quantity is computed from reconstructed magnetic-flux contours, usually constrained by external magnetic probes, flux loops, and internal measurements such as motional-Stark-effect polarimetry when available. Contour extraction from [poloidal magnetic flux](name:poloidal_magnetic_flux) surfaces provides the required inner, outer, and uppermost locations.

Typical values are 0-0.6 for conventional positively shaped tokamak plasmas, can approach 0.8 in strongly shaped scenarios, and are negative for inverse-triangularity upper shaping.

candidates.description: Total pressure of an ion species including thermal and non-thermal contributions
candidates.reason: qualifier=ion, base=pressure; total is implied by unsplit ion pressure
candidates.docs_description: One-third trace of the ion pressure tensor, representing random kinetic stress from ion motion in plasma momentum balance.
candidates.documentation:
Ion pressure is the scalar isotropic part of the kinetic pressure tensor of the ion population, equal to one third of its trace. It supplies the ion contribution to the total plasma pressure and enters force balance through pressure-gradient terms. In a multi-species plasma it is the sum over the included ion species; [ion_density](name:ion_density) and [ion_temperature](name:ion_temperature) determine the Maxwellian limit, while [electron_pressure](name:electron_pressure) gives the corresponding electron contribution.

The kinetic definition is

$$
p_i = \frac{1}{3}\sum_s m_s \int |\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{u}_s|^2 f_s(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{v})\,d^3v
$$

where $p_i$ is the ion pressure in Pa, $s$ labels ion species, $m_s$ is ion mass in kg, $\mathbf{x}$ is position in m, $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{u}_s$ are particle and mean-flow velocities in m s$^{-1}$, and $f_s$ is normalized so $\int f_s d^3v=n_s$ with $n_s$ in m$^{-3}$. For Maxwellian ions this reduces to $p_s=n_s k_B T_s$, or $p_s=n_s e T_s$ when $T_s$ is expressed in eV.

Ion pressure is obtained by combining ion-temperature measurements from charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy, neutral-particle analysis, or Doppler broadening with ion density inferred from interferometry, Thomson scattering plus quasineutrality, or species-balance modeling. Equilibrium and transport calculations use it together with [plasma_pressure](name:plasma_pressure) to close pressure balance and energy transport.

Representative values span about 1-200 kPa in tokamak cores, rising with density and ion temperature, and about 0.01-10 kPa near the pedestal and edge.

candidates.description: Ratio of volume-averaged plasma pressure to poloidal magnetic pressure
candidates.docs_description: Dimensionless measure of plasma thermal pressure relative to the magnetic pressure of the confining poloidal field.
candidates.documentation:
Poloidal beta measures how strongly plasma pressure loads the equilibrium supported by the poloidal magnetic field. It is larger than the corresponding toroidal-field beta in tokamaks because the poloidal field is much weaker than the toroidal field, and it is closely tied to Shafranov shift, pressure peaking, and current-profile effects.

A common tokamak definition is

$$
\beta_p = \frac{2\mu_0 \langle p \rangle}{B_{p,\mathrm{ref}}^2}
$$

where $\mu_0$ is the vacuum permeability, $\langle p \rangle$ is the volume-averaged plasma pressure, and $B_{p,\mathrm{ref}}$ is a reference edge or boundary poloidal magnetic-field strength. The pressure entering this ratio is related to [plasma pressure](name:plasma_pressure), while the denominator is determined from the [poloidal magnetic field](name:poloidal_magnetic_field).

Poloidal beta is inferred from equilibrium reconstruction constrained by magnetic diagnostics, plasma current, and kinetic pressure measurements such as Thomson scattering, charge-exchange spectroscopy, and interferometry. It is often analyzed together with [plasma current](name:plasma_current), [internal inductance](name:internal_inductance), and [normalized beta](name:normalized_beta) because these quantities jointly determine force balance and stability margins.

Representative values are about 0.1-1 in low-pressure or Ohmic tokamak plasmas, 0.5-3 in high-performance H-mode scenarios, and several units in spherical tokamaks or advanced high-pressure regimes.

candidates.description: Local radial shear of the safety factor on magnetic flux surfaces
candidates.reason: base=magnetic_shear; radial dependence is coordinate context
candidates.docs_description: Radial variation of field-line helicity on nested magnetic flux surfaces, measuring how rapidly neighboring field lines change pitch.
candidates.documentation:
Magnetic shear describes the radial change of magnetic-field-line pitch across nested flux surfaces. It is the logarithmic radial derivative of the [safety factor](name:safety_factor), so it controls how rapidly neighboring field lines separate in poloidal angle as they wind toroidally. Low or reversed shear is important for internal transport barriers and tearing-mode stability, while large edge shear affects ballooning and peeling stability.

For an outward-increasing flux-surface radial coordinate $\rho$, the local magnetic shear $\hat{s}$ is defined by:

$$
\hat{s}(\rho) = \frac{\rho}{q(\rho)}\frac{dq}{d\rho} = \frac{d\ln |q|}{d\ln \rho}
$$

where $q(\rho)$ is the safety factor on that surface. Equivalent forms may use a flux label derived from [poloidal magnetic flux](name:poloidal_magnetic_flux) or [toroidal magnetic flux](name:toroidal_magnetic_flux), provided the derivative is transformed consistently.

Magnetic shear is inferred from equilibrium reconstruction constrained by external magnetic measurements, polarimetry, and motional Stark effect observations; it is not measured directly by a single diagnostic. Typical tokamak values are about 0-1 in the core for monotonic-shear equilibria, 1-5 near the edge, and -2-0 in reversed-shear regions near the minimum of $|q|$.

Sign convention: Positive when the magnitude of the safety factor increases from the magnetic axis toward the plasma boundary.

candidates.description: Signed ratio of toroidal to poloidal field-line turns on a flux surface
candidates.reason: base=safety_factor; radial dependence is coordinate sampling
candidates.docs_description: Number of toroidal transits a magnetic field line makes for one poloidal circuit on a magnetic flux surface.
candidates.documentation:
The safety factor $q$ measures magnetic-field-line helicity on a nested magnetic flux surface. It connects the winding of field lines to equilibrium stability: low-order rational values can resonate with magnetohydrodynamic modes, while the radial variation of $q$ determines [magnetic shear](name:magnetic_shear).

For an axisymmetric equilibrium, $q$ is defined by the ratio of toroidal to poloidal magnetic-flux increments between neighboring flux surfaces:

$$
q(\psi) = \frac{d\Phi_{\mathrm{tor}}}{d\Phi_{\mathrm{pol}}}
$$

where $\psi$ labels the flux surface, $\Phi_{\mathrm{tor}}$ is the [toroidal magnetic flux](name:toroidal_magnetic_flux), and $\Phi_{\mathrm{pol}}$ is the [poloidal magnetic flux](name:poloidal_magnetic_flux). Equivalent field-line definitions count the toroidal angle advance per full poloidal circuit.

The safety factor is computed from magnetic-equilibrium reconstruction constrained by external magnetic measurements, internal magnetic-pitch measurements such as motional Stark effect, polarimetry, and kinetic pressure information. In tokamak H-mode plasmas, representative values are $q_0 \approx 0.8$-$1.5$ near the magnetic axis and $q_{95} \approx 3$-$5$ near the outer confined region; reversed-shear and advanced scenarios can have larger central values or off-axis minima.

candidates.description: Number density of electrons including thermal and non-thermal populations
candidates.reason: qualifier=electron, base=density; total over thermal and fast populations
candidates.docs_description: Number of free electrons per unit volume in the plasma, including all electron populations that contribute to quasineutrality.
candidates.documentation:
Electron density is the local number density of free electrons and is a primary state variable for transport, radiation, collisionality, and wave propagation. In a quasineutral plasma it is constrained by the ion charge balance, so it is closely related to [ion_density](name:ion_density), while its product with [electron_temperature](name:electron_temperature) sets [electron_pressure](name:electron_pressure).

For a kinetic electron distribution, the density $n_e$ is the zeroth velocity moment of the electron distribution function $f_e(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{v})$:

$$
n_e(\mathbf{x}) = \int f_e(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{v})\,d^3v
$$

where $\mathbf{x}$ is position, $\mathbf{v}$ is particle velocity, and $f_e$ is defined so that integration over velocity space gives particles per unit volume. For a quasineutral plasma, $n_e \simeq \sum_i Z_i n_i$, where $Z_i$ and $n_i$ are the charge number and density of ion species $i$.

Electron density is measured with Thomson scattering, interferometry, polarimetry, reflectometry, and spectroscopy-based inversions. Transport analysis combines these diagnostics with equilibrium geometry to determine the spatial variation used in particle balance, impurity screening, and current-drive calculations.

Typical values range from $10^{19}$ to $2 \times 10^{20}\;\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ in the confined plasma of present tokamaks, with lower scrape-off-layer values near $10^{17}$-$10^{19}\;\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ and higher transient edge values during fueling or detachment.

candidates.description: Total electron pressure including thermal and non-thermal contributions
candidates.reason: qualifier=electron, base=pressure; total distinguishes from thermal sibling
candidates.docs_description: Kinetic pressure exerted by the electron population, representing the isotropic momentum flux associated with electron random motion.
candidates.documentation:
Electron pressure is the scalar isotropic part of the electron pressure tensor and measures the momentum flux carried by random electron motion. It enters force balance, drift dynamics, and energy transport through gradients that couple to electric fields and magnetic geometry, making it a key companion to [electron density](name:electron_density) and [electron temperature](name:electron_temperature).

For a Maxwellian electron population, the pressure is related to density and temperature by

$$
p_e = n_e k_B T_e
$$

where $p_e$ is electron pressure, $n_e$ is electron number density, $T_e$ is electron temperature in kelvin, and $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant. When $T_e$ is expressed in eV, the equivalent relation is $p_e = n_e e T_e$, where $e$ is the elementary charge.

Electron pressure is computed from measurements of electron density and temperature, most often from Thomson scattering, interferometry combined with temperature diagnostics, electron cyclotron emission, or integrated transport analysis. Its spatial gradient is closely related to [electron_pressure_gradient](name:electron_pressure_gradient), which drives diamagnetic drifts and contributes to pressure-gradient terms in transport equations.

Common values range from about 10-1,000 Pa near the plasma edge to 1,000-100,000 Pa in high-performance tokamak cores, depending mainly on density, temperature, and confinement regime.

candidates.description: Toroidal magnetic field evaluated at the magnetic axis
candidates.docs_description: Toroidal component of the equilibrium magnetic field evaluated on the magnetic axis, setting the local reference field for gyro-motion and confinement.
candidates.documentation:
The toroidal magnetic field at the magnetic axis is the projection of the equilibrium magnetic-field vector onto the toroidal basis direction at the center of the nested flux surfaces. It is the local reference value used for gyrofrequency estimates, magnetic rigidity, and comparison with the broader [toroidal magnetic field](name:toroidal_magnetic_field) distribution.

In the right-handed cylindrical $(R, \phi, Z)$ frame, where $R$ is major radius, $\phi$ is toroidal angle, and $Z$ is vertical height, the quantity is defined by

$$
B_{\phi 0} = \mathbf{B}(R_{\mathrm{axis}}, Z_{\mathrm{axis}}) \cdot \hat{\phi}
$$

where $\mathbf{B}$ is the equilibrium magnetic-field vector, $\hat{\phi}$ is the toroidal unit vector, and $(R_{\mathrm{axis}}, Z_{\mathrm{axis}})$ locates the [magnetic axis](name:major_radius_of_magnetic_axis) in the poloidal plane. The field magnitude near the axis also enters the ion gyrofrequency through $\Omega_i = Z_i e |B_{\phi 0}|/m_i$, where $Z_i$ is ion charge state, $e$ is the elementary charge, and $m_i$ is ion mass.

This quantity is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction constrained by external magnetic measurements, polarimetry, and motional-Stark-effect measurements, together with calibrated toroidal-field coil currents. Direct local measurement at the magnetic axis is not available in most toroidal devices, so the value is inferred from the reconstructed field and the reconstructed magnetic-axis position.

Typical values are 1-3 T in medium-size tokamaks, 3-6 T in high-field tokamaks, and can exceed 10 T in compact high-field concepts. It is closely related to [magnetic field magnitude](name:magnetic_field_magnitude) at the axis when the poloidal field is small compared with the toroidal component.

Sign convention: Positive when the toroidal field points in the direction of increasing toroidal angle $\phi$.

candidates.description: Total ion number density including thermal and nonthermal contributions
candidates.reason: qualifier=ion, base=density; unit indicates number density
candidates.docs_description: Number of ions per unit volume in a plasma, summed over ion populations and charge states when no species resolution is specified.
candidates.documentation:
Ion density is the number density of ion particles in the plasma, including all ion charge states and populations represented by the total ion population. It differs from [electron_density](name:electron_density) because quasineutrality weights each ion species by charge state, so $n_e \simeq \sum_s Z_s n_s$ while the total ion count is an unweighted particle sum.

For ion species or charge-state populations indexed by $s$, the total ion density $n_i$ is defined as

$$
n_i = \sum_s n_s
$$

where $n_s$ is the number density of ion population $s$ and $Z_s$ is its charge number when charge neutrality is evaluated. The ion pressure relation is then population dependent; for a single Maxwellian ion population, [ion_pressure](name:ion_pressure) is related to [ion_temperature](name:ion_temperature) through $p_i = n_i k_B T_i$, with $k_B$ the Boltzmann constant and $T_i$ the ion temperature.

Ion density is inferred from a combination of diagnostics and modeling: interferometry and Thomson scattering constrain the electron density, charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy and spectroscopy constrain impurity and main-ion concentrations, and quasineutrality closes the ion balance. In deuterium-tritium core plasmas, representative values are about $0.3-1.5 \times 10^{20}\;\mathrm{m^{-3}}$, while edge and scrape-off-layer values can fall to $10^{17}-10^{19}\;\mathrm{m^{-3}}$ depending on fueling and recycling. The density is non-negative by definition and has no coordinate-dependent sign convention.

candidates.description: Vertical-to-horizontal half-axis ratio of a flux surface
candidates.docs_description: Ratio of a flux surface's vertical Z half-extent to its radial R half-width in the right-handed cylindrical (R, phi, Z) frame.
candidates.documentation:
Elongation is a dimensionless shaping parameter for a magnetic flux surface in a poloidal cross-section. In the right-handed cylindrical $(R, \phi, Z)$ frame, $R$ is major radius, $\phi$ is toroidal angle, and $Z$ is vertical height; elongation measures how stretched the closed contour is along $Z$ relative to $R$.

For a closed flux-surface contour, the common geometric definition is

$$
\kappa = \frac{Z_{\max}-Z_{\min}}{R_{\max}-R_{\min}}
$$

where $Z_{\max}$ and $Z_{\min}$ are the upper and lower vertical extrema of the contour, and $R_{\max}$ and $R_{\min}$ are its outer and inner radial extrema. This definition connects directly to other shaping descriptors such as [triangularity of the flux surface](name:triangularity_of_flux_surface) and [squareness of the flux surface](name:squareness_of_flux_surface).

Elongation is computed from equilibrium reconstruction by identifying contours of [poloidal magnetic flux](name:poloidal_magnetic_flux), often constrained by magnetic probes, flux loops, plasma-boundary imaging, and internal magnetic-angle measurements when available. Representative values are 1 for circular cross-sections, 1.5-2.0 in many elongated tokamak plasmas, and up to about 2.5 in strongly shaped scenarios; increasing elongation improves confinement and stability limits but raises demands on vertical-position control.

docs_quality_scores.name: electron_pressure
docs_quality_scores.reasoning: Description quality (17): The single-line description is clean and precise about the isotropic kinetic-pressure nature, but it misses the defining feature in the DD ground truth — that this is the TOTAL electron pressure including the fast/non-thermal contribution. Documentation quality (18): The defining equation p_e = n_e k_B T_e is in correct LaTeX with all variables defined and units (Pa) consistent; the eV-form equivalent p_e = n_e e T_e is also correct and well explained. Cross-references to electron_density, electron_temperature, and electron_pressure_gradient are appropriate, and diagnostic context (Thomson, ECE, interferometry) is sound. No siblings were available for terminology comparison. Completeness (17): Covers definition, equation, diagnostic inference, typical magnitudes, and cross-references, but does not mention the fast-electron contribution that distinguishes 'total electron pressure' from purely thermal pressure — a gap relative to the DD scope. Physics accuracy (17): All stated equations balance dimensionally and the typical value ranges (10–10^5 Pa) are physically reasonable for tokamak edge to core. The only divergence is the implicit framing of p_e as a Maxwellian/thermal quantity, whereas the DD defines it as total pressure combining thermal and non-thermal electrons; this is an understatement of scope rather than a contradiction. No implementation leakage. Total 69/80, accept.
